w:r.w:t: What is done · Where we are · How to build from here
1. The One Thing to Understand First
Everything in this document is based on one decision: Antigravity is the only development environment. Code lives there, runs there, and is tested there. Nothing else is used for coding.
Here is what each tool does in this project:
The development environment. All code lives here. All testing happens here. The evermaze folder is permanent.
The planning partner. Writes system prompts. Reviews results. Answers questions. Does NOT write code directly.
No longer used for this project. It created a download/upload loop that wasted time. Removed from workflow.
The law. Every game rule, every mechanic, every edge case. If anything conflicts with the GDD — the GDD wins.

Google AI Studio Apps is no longer part of this workflow. It was used for the test project only. From Module 02 onward, all code is written directly inside Antigravity using its built-in AI assistant.
31 pages. Production ready. This is the source of truth for every mechanic, every rule, every edge case. Do not change anything in the game design without updating the GDD first.

Key contents: three-phase match flow, maze generation rules, Lost Light partial information system, all 7 gifts with full specs, exit channel mechanic, Sudden Death, all 30+ edge cases resolved, sound and visual design, onboarding, technical architecture, MVP scope, build plan, market strategy.
Defines the 25-module build structure. One module per session. Each module owns one subsystem. No module begins until the previous one is verified. This document follows that structure.

Contains everything an AI assistant needs to understand the project: role, tech stack, folder structure, naming conventions, all constants, all event names, architecture rules, code quality standards. It is pasted at the start of every AI session inside Antigravity.

All 4 pass/fail checks confirmed working. Two terminals run simultaneously. WebSocket stays connected. Key finding: no public URL without Ngrok — Ngrok will be added in Module 04 for real-device testing.
The evermaze folder exists in Antigravity with this structure:
All Socket.io event names, all game config constants, all enums (TILE, PHASE, TEAM, GIFT) — frozen objects
Express + Socket.io server entry point, /health endpoint, graceful shutdown
Stub — placeholder only, replaced in Module 02
Stub — placeholder only, replaced in Module 02
Three-view shell: landing / lobby / game — placeholder text
Module 01 is the skeleton. Nothing runs as a real game yet. It is the correct base to build Module 02 on top of.
Module 01 is complete and verified. Module 02 has NOT been built yet. We are starting Module 02 fresh, inside Antigravity, using Antigravity's built-in AI. This is the correct starting point.
4. The Workflow — How Every Module Is Built
This is the complete operating procedure. Follow it for every module from 02 to 25 without exception.
Step 1 — Open Antigravity and go to the evermaze folder
All work happens inside the evermaze folder. Never create a new folder for a new module. Modules add files to the same folder or replace existing files within it.
Step 2 — Open Antigravity's built-in AI assistant
Antigravity has an AI coding assistant built in. This replaces Google AI Studio entirely. You use this AI to generate code for each module.
Step 3 — Start with the Master Prompt

Every AI session — without exception — begins by pasting the full contents of MASTER_PROMPT.md into the Antigravity AI assistant. This gives it the full project context: architecture, naming rules, constants, event names, code standards.
Never skip the Master Prompt. An AI session without it does not know the project rules, will name things wrong, and will create code that breaks other modules.
Step 4 — Paste the module system prompt

Immediately after the Master Prompt, paste the system prompt for the current module. The system prompt tells the AI exactly what to build: which files to create, which files to modify, the functional requirements, the edge cases, and the testing checklist.
Step 5 — Wait for confirmation before code
After pasting both prompts, the AI must respond with a confirmation listing:
The module name and number it is building
The exact files it will create or modify
Any questions or ambiguities before it starts

If the AI skips this and starts writing code immediately — type "Stop. List the files you are about to create before writing any code." Do not accept code without a scope confirmation first. This prevents the AI from building outside its assigned module.
Compare the AI's listed files against the SCOPE section of the system prompt. If they match — type "Confirmed. Proceed."
If they do not match — correct it before code is written. Example: "File X is not in the scope. Remove it from your plan and confirm again."
The AI writes each file. Because you are inside Antigravity, you apply each file directly:
If the file does not exist yet: create it at the correct path and paste the content
If the file already exists (a stub from Module 01): open it and replace all content with the new version
Do not modify the code before applying it unless there is an obvious syntax error
Step 8 — Run npm install if needed
If the module added new packages to package.json, run these in the Antigravity terminals:
Terminal 1: cd server && npm install && npm run dev
Terminal 2: cd client && npm install && npm run dev
If no new packages were added, just restart both servers.
Every system prompt ends with a testing checklist of 8–12 items. Run every single one manually inside Antigravity.
Open the preview URL in a browser tab
Open a second tab for multiplayer tests
Each item: PASS or FAIL — no partial credit
All items must PASS before the next module begins. If one item fails, the module is not complete. Fix it first.
Step 10 — Fix failures in the same session
If a test fails, stay in the same Antigravity AI session and type:
"Testing checklist item [number] is failing. Exact error: [paste error here]. Fix only this issue. Do not change files outside the current module scope."
Apply the fix. Re-run only the failing test. Repeat until it passes.
Come back to this conversation and report using this exact format:
Module [number] — [name]: COMPLETE All [N] checklist items: PASS Errors encountered and fixed: [describe or "none"] Files created or modified: [list them] Ready for: Module [next number]
Step 12 — Receive the next system prompt from Claude

Claude writes each system prompt only after the previous module is verified. This ensures each prompt is written with full knowledge of what was actually built. You will receive the next system prompt here, copy it, and use it in Antigravity for the next session.
System prompts for modules 03–25 are written one at a time by Claude. They are not pre-written. This is intentional — each prompt depends on what was actually built before it.
This is the current module. Use the system prompt below inside Antigravity AI to build it. Follow the workflow in Section 4 exactly.
Module 02 builds the complete server-side room lifecycle. After this module, the server can:
Create a room and return a unique 6-character code
Allow players to join a room by code
Enforce team balance (cannot join team if it creates 2+ player gap)
Handle players marking themselves as Ready
Allow the host to start the game
Handle disconnections gracefully (60-second rejoin window, host migration)
Clean up rooms after 2 hours of inactivity
Module 02 is backend only. No UI. No canvas. No game logic. Room lifecycle only. The frontend cannot see any of this yet — that comes in Modules 03 and 04.
generateRoomCode, generatePlayerId, tileDistance, withinRadius, sanitizePlayer, sanitizeRoomForBroadcast
Room state factory function, team count helpers, balance check, allPlayersReady, findNextHost
Match state factory — complete state shape initialized to defaults (game logic comes in later modules)
Full RoomManager class: createRoom, joinRoom, leaveRoom, handleDisconnect, rejoinRoom, switchTeam, toggleReady, startGame, migrateHost, kickPlayer, deleteRoom, getStats
Full event handlers for all lobby events with validation and error handling
Pass roomManager to registerHandlers, update /health to use getStats()
Add ROOM_UPDATE, SWITCH_TEAM, KICK_PLAYER event names
After building, verify every item manually in Antigravity:
cd server && npm run dev — no errors in terminal
localhost:3001/health returns { status: ok, rooms: 0, players: 0, timestamp: ... }
Second browser tab joins and receives full room state
Returns a clear error message — server does not crash
Third player auto-joins the smaller team — cannot join the larger team
Toggles correctly — ROOM_UPDATE broadcasts to all connected tabs
When host disconnects, PLAYER_LEFT event includes newHostId — a different player is now host
When last player leaves, /health shows rooms: 0 — room was deleted
Create 5 rooms rapidly — all have different codes
Copy everything between the lines below and paste it into Antigravity AI after the Master Prompt:
Paste this immediately after the Master Prompt. Do not add anything else between them.
You are building the complete backend foundation for The Ever Maze. This module owns the room lifecycle and player management. You replace the stub files from Module 01 with full implementations.

Produce a fully working Node.js + Socket.io backend where players can create a room, join by code, choose a team, set ready status, and the host can start a game. All GDD Section 12 rules apply. No maze generation. No game logic. Room lifecycle only.
SCOPE — CREATE OR MODIFY EXACTLY THESE FILES:
DO NOT touch: client/ folder, any other server files not listed above.
generateRoomCode() — 6-char alphanumeric, excludes 0/O/I/l, unique per call
generatePlayerId() — UUID v4 using the uuid package
clamp(value, min, max) — clamps a number
tileDistance(posA, posB) — Euclidean distance between {x,y} positions
sanitizePlayer(player) — returns player object with socketId removed
sanitizeRoomForBroadcast(room) — returns room safe for client broadcast
Export createRoom(hostSocketId, hostName, settings) returning a room object with these fields:

code (string), createdAt (number), hostId (string), players (object keyed by socketId), settings (matchDuration, mazeSize, giftFrequency, wallShifts), match (null), status ("lobby"), inactivityTimerId (null)
Each player object inside players has: id, name, team (null until chosen), isReady, isHost, isConnected, disconnectedAt, position (null), gift (null), hasPickupImmunity, isAfk, afkTimerId
Also export: getTeamCount(room, team), wouldImbalance(room, team), allPlayersReady(room), getConnectedPlayers(room), hasValidHost(room), findNextHost(room)
Export createMatchState(room, maze) returning a match state object with these fields initialized to defaults:

startedAt, duration, phase (PHASE.EXPLORE), phaseStartedAt, maze (null), lostLight ({carrierId:null, position:null, isOnFloor:true, foundAt:null, transferCount:0, pickupImmunityUntil:null}), gifts ([]), wallShifts ({count:0, nextShiftAt:null, warningActive:false, pendingShiftZone:null}), exit ({position:null, openedAt:null}), channel ({playerId:null, startedAt:null, progress:0, interrupted:false}), suddenDeath ({active:false, startedAt:null}), tickCount (0), lastTickAt (null), result (null)
Replace the stub with a full RoomManager class. Properties: rooms (new Map()), socketToRoom (new Map()).
All methods return { success: boolean, error?: string, data?: any } — never throw.

createRoom(socketId, playerName, settings) — validates name (2–20 chars), generates unique code (retry up to 10 times), creates room, assigns host to TEAM.A, adds to both maps, starts 2-hour inactivity timer
joinRoom(socketId, roomCode, playerName) — validates code exists, room is in lobby status, under 14 players, auto-assigns to smaller team, enforces balance
leaveRoom(socketId) — removes player, triggers host migration if needed, deletes room if empty
handleDisconnect(socketId) — sets isConnected:false, starts REJOIN_WINDOW timer, does NOT remove player immediately
rejoinRoom(socketId, roomCode, playerName) — matches by name+code, restores connection
switchTeam(socketId, team) — lobby only, balance enforced
startGame(socketId) — validates host + all ready + min 2 players + lobby status, sets status to "starting"
migrateHost(roomCode) — finds next valid host via findNextHost(), updates hostId and player.isHost
kickPlayer(hostSocketId, targetSocketId) — host only, cannot kick self
deleteRoom(roomCode) — clears all timers, removes from both maps

AFK detection: on any PLAYER_MOVE event (even though movement is not built yet — wire the reset now), reset the AFK timer. On no movement for AFK_WARNING ms: emit a warning to that socket. If no input within AFK_KICK ms: call leaveRoom.
Replace stub with registerHandlers(io, roomManager). Handle all events with try/catch and callback responses.
EVENTS.CREATE_ROOM — validate name, call createRoom, socket.join(roomCode), emit EVENTS.ROOM_JOINED to caller with {roomCode, player, room}, broadcast EVENTS.PLAYER_JOINED to others
EVENTS.JOIN_ROOM — validate code (uppercase it) and name, call joinRoom, same join/broadcast pattern
EVENTS.LEAVE_ROOM — call leaveRoom, socket.leave, broadcast EVENTS.PLAYER_LEFT with {playerId, newHostId}
EVENTS.PLAYER_READY — call toggleReady, broadcast EVENTS.ROOM_UPDATE with sanitized room
EVENTS.START_GAME — call startGame, broadcast EVENTS.PHASE_CHANGE with {phase: PHASE.EXPLORE, message: "Match starting"}
KICK_PLAYER — call kickPlayer, emit EVENTS.ROOM_ERROR to kicked socket, broadcast EVENTS.PLAYER_LEFT
"disconnect" — call handleDisconnect, broadcast EVENTS.PLAYER_LEFT with {playerId, isTemporary:true, newHostId}

Import RoomManager. Instantiate: const roomManager = new RoomManager(). Pass to registerHandlers: registerHandlers(io, roomManager). Update /health: return { status: "ok", ...roomManager.getStats(), timestamp: Date.now() }
Add these to the EVENTS object: ROOM_UPDATE: "ROOM_UPDATE", SWITCH_TEAM: "SWITCH_TEAM", KICK_PLAYER: "KICK_PLAYER"
Duplicate room code on creation — retry generateRoomCode up to 10 times
Player joins full room (14 players) — error: "Room is full"
Player joins active match — error: "Match already in progress"
Host tries to kick themselves — error: "Cannot kick yourself"
Player already in a room tries to join another — error: "Already in a room"
Carrier disconnects with Lost Light — handled in Module 10, but do not crash here
All RoomManager methods return result objects — never throw exceptions
socketToRoom map stays perfectly in sync with rooms map at all times
All timers (AFK, inactivity, rejoin) stored on room/player and cleared in deleteRoom/leaveRoom
No hardcoded event strings — all use EVENTS.* from shared/constants.js
No hardcoded timeout values — all use GAME_CONFIG.* from shared/constants.js
Try/catch on every socket event handler
All 7 files in scope are present in output
socketToRoom updated in every path that adds or removes a player
All timers cleared in deleteRoom and leaveRoom
No hardcoded strings or numbers outside shared/constants.js
ROOM_UPDATE, SWITCH_TEAM, KICK_PLAYER added to shared/constants.js
STOP CONDITION: Output all 7 files. Then stop. Do not begin Module 03. Do not add maze generation. Do not add game loop. Wait for review.
The section above between the two horizontal lines is the complete system prompt for Module 02. Copy it exactly and paste it after the Master Prompt in Antigravity AI.
6. All Future Modules — What Each One Builds

These modules are built in strict order. Each depends on all previous ones being complete. System prompts for each will be written by Claude after the previous module is verified. This section gives you the overview so you know what is coming.
End goal: A working lobby. Two people on different devices can create a room, join, pick teams, and start a match.
Folder structure, constants, enums, skeleton files
Room lifecycle, player management, host migration, AFK
React views wired to server, socket events handled, gameState updating
Full lobby UI, team selection, ready states, Ngrok for real device testing
End goal: A procedurally generated maze renders in the browser with fog of war. Different every match.
Recursive Backtracker algorithm, BFS balance validation, gift tile placement, shift zones, exit tiles, emergency fallback library
HTML5 Canvas 60fps renderer: tiles, player markers, gift orbs, Lost Light glow, minimap
Fog of war overlay (6-tile radius), explored tile memory, minimap dots, Level 3 carrier proximity reveal
End goal: Players move through the maze in real time. Movement feels smooth and responsive.
WASD input capture, movement sent to server, client-side prediction, server reconciliation, wall collision, carrier 90% speed
20-tick server game loop, STATE_UPDATE broadcast, client interpolation between snapshots, input rate limiting, reconnection
End goal: A full match can be played. Lost Light found, hunted, carried to exit, team wins.
Spawn, pickup, 2s immunity, carrier tracking, three-tier partial information, transfer on tag, floor drop on disconnect
1.5-tile radius, Bresenham line-of-sight, server timestamp resolution, all restrictions (friendly fire, cooldown, immunity, Shield)
All 7 gifts: Freeze, Dash, Mist, Compass, Magic Key, Shield, Silent Steps — full effects and counter interactions
Phase 1→2 transition: golden flash sequence (exact GDD timing), amber pulse, awakening sound, phase state machine
Phase 2 only, max 3 shifts, 3s amber warning, player proximity check, BFS validation, postpone logic
Phase 3 trigger at 70%, exit tile selection, 3-second channel, interrupt on tag, Sudden Death, draw resolution
End goal: The game looks and sounds complete. A new player understands what to do without any instructions.
Match timer, gift slot, phase indicator, onboarding overlays, post-match summary, landing page
Web Audio API — all 17 sound events procedurally generated (no audio files needed)
Screen flash, wall root pulse, tag burst, channel progress bar, exit gate animation, Sudden Death reveal
WCAG AA contrast, non-color signals, colorblind mode, font scaling, key prompt icons
End goal: The game is tested, optimized, live at a public URL, and people are playing it.
Automated tests: maze generation (10 runs), all RoomManager methods, full 4v4 match simulation, all 30+ GDD edge cases
Canvas dirty rect, delta STATE_UPDATE, code splitting, memory leak verification, < 3s load time target
ESLint zero errors, JSDoc on all exports, server authority audit, no magic numbers anywhere
Vercel (frontend), Railway (backend), custom domain, SSL, CORS tightened to production
4 real players on different devices/networks, all Antigravity test checks re-run on production URLs
r/WebGames, r/indiegaming, itch.io, Product Hunt, public playtest session
Never ask Antigravity AI to build two modules in one session. Each module is designed to be small enough to build and fully verify individually.
A module is complete when every checklist item passes — not when the AI says it is done. Manual verification is required.
If the AI suggests a different approach to any mechanic — check the GDD first. If the GDD specifies it, implement it as specified. Do not let the AI redesign mechanics.
Every system prompt lists exact files to create or modify. If the AI creates a file not on that list — reject it. Do not apply it.
The client never makes game decisions. It sends input and renders what the server returns. Any code that lets the client set game state is a bug.
No magic numbers or hardcoded strings anywhere except shared/constants.js. If you see a raw number like 3000 or a raw string like PLAYER_MOVE in any other file — that is wrong.
If the AI writes a function that returns null, throws "not implemented", or has a TODO — it is not done. Make it implement the function fully.
Every single Antigravity AI session starts with the Master Prompt pasted first. No exceptions.
Stay in same session. Type: "Checklist item [N] failing with error: [paste error]. Fix this only without changing files outside scope."
AI creates a file not in scope
Do not apply it. Type: "File [name] is not in scope. Remove it from your output."
AI writes code without confirming scope
Type: "Stop. List all files you will create or modify before writing any code."
Check CLIENT_ORIGIN env var in server matches frontend URL exactly (including port number).
Antigravity free tier may timeout. Restart the server terminal. Consider Railway for backend if this is persistent.
socketToRoom map is out of sync. Check deleteRoom() clears both maps. Check leaveRoom() calls deleteRoom when room is empty.
Cannot figure out what the current file contains
The files in Antigravity are the source of truth. Open the file directly to see its current contents.
Not sure which module to build next
Check Section 3.1 of this document. The row marked BUILD NOW is the current module.
cd server && npm install && npm run dev
cd client && npm install && npm run dev
Terminal 3 — only needed from Module 04 onward
Module [number] — [name]: COMPLETE All [N] checklist items: PASS Errors encountered and fixed: [describe or "none"] Files modified: [list them] Ready for: Module [next number] system prompt
The Ever Maze — Agent Guidance v3.0
Antigravity is the development environment. One module at a time. Verify before continuing.
